problem_summary: 2021 MCM/ICM Problem E (sustainability): redesign the global food system to better balance sustainability and equity against the current system's priority on efficiency/profitability. The team had to model how a re-optimized food system would change food output, quantify the costs/benefits of the transition, compare effects on a developed vs. developing nation, and account for population growth over time. This O-award paper (control number 2113869) splits the system into a production side (a BP neural-network "Production Model" over planting/husbandry/fisheries) and a distribution side (a Dijkstra-based "Equitable Distribution Model" that pairs surplus donor countries with Low-Income Food-Deficit Countries via minimum transport distance), then layers a time-dimension "Growth Model" and a chemical-pollution accounting scheme on top.
models_used: BP (back-propagation) neural network as the core 'Production Model' — single hidden layer of 8 neurons, ReLU activation; trained on 782 samples from 30 countries (1993-2018), 70/30 train/test split, MAE tracked vs. epoch (Fig 5)

Three parallel per-industry production sub-models (planting, husbandry, fisheries), each a separate NN with industry-specific input features; output values summed across industries to a single 'total food production value' (units unified via value not volume)
Production-capacity inference trick: feed each country's REAL labor & ASTI but the LIMIT/criterion value of chemicals and intensity index to estimate each country's MAXIMUM potential output

Dijkstra shortest-path algorithm for the 'Distance Minimization Model' — pairs 10 donor countries (highest food possession per capita) with 18 UN-designated least-developed beneficiary countries by minimizing total transport distance (Fig 14 donor-receiver matching)
Food possession per capita = max food production value / population, used to rank/select the 10 donor countries (Fig 11)
Growth Model: time-series extrapolation of ASTI (tech) to 2100 (Fig 15) and population to 2100 from UN World Population Prospects (Fig 16), fed back into the NN to make production dynamic

Linear weighted chemical-pollution accounting model: Air/Water/Land = Σ over 3 industries of (w1+w2)*Chemicals_i, with element-composition-based coefficients (Table 3) splitting CO2/noxious-gas, inorganic/organic contamination

Food Donations Function: donation volume as a function of production value, GDP growth/business cycle, and participation rate in international aid projects (Fig 23) — bridges 'output value' to 'donation tonnage' for transport-cost calc

Criterion-value policy mechanism: defines per-industry 'criterion' Intensity Index (Table 1: 6.0/4.7/5.3) and chemical-utilization (Table 2: 251.5/150.9/75.4) set to the average of the most-developed countries as sustainability targets policies push toward
key_techniques: Decomposition of a vague 'system' into three concrete, separately-modeled sub-industries (planting/husbandry/fisheries) with tailored feature sets per industry

Converting policy levers into model inputs: sustainability policies are operationalized as changes to two NN input variables (Chemicals, Intensity Index), so 'curb overproduction' and 'restrict chemical abuse' become numeric perturbations whose output effect is directly readable
Using value (dollars) instead of volume to make heterogeneous food types additive across industries — then re-deriving volume only where needed (transport) via the Food Donations Function
Proxy-variable substitution where direct data is unavailable: husbandry feed access/quality is unobservable, so planting development is used as a substitute variable

Two-country contrast (USA developed vs. Nepal developing) as a concrete validation/illustration vehicle, with quantified deltas (USA planting/husbandry/fishery -2.1%/-1.0%/-1.2%; Nepal +5.3%/+2.9%/+1.4%)

Sensitivity/scalability test on 20 held-out countries split by land area, computing predicted error rate by group (Fig 27) and honestly reporting that large-area countries fit worse (~10-20% error vs ~2-10%)
Heatmap sweep over the two policy levers (chemical use x intensity) to show the output-value response surface (Fig 19) rather than a single point estimate
End-to-end worked numeric example: USA -> Haiti (700 km) + Guatemala (1754 km), donation volume 120,500 tons -> 20.55M USD transport cost via cost = unit price x distance x volume
why_it_won: "From a judge's view it wins on completeness and on closing the loop between the model and the problem's actual asks. It (1) covers every prompt requirement — re-optimized output, costs/benefits, developed-vs-developing comparison, and a future/time dimension — with a dedicated model component for each; (2) makes the sustainability and equity abstractions tractable by reducing them to numeric levers (chemicals, intensity index, transport distance) the model can actually move; (3) delivers concrete, quoted numbers (percentage output changes, 20.55M transport cost, criterion tables, 2100 projections) instead of hand-waving, which reads as 'this team actually computed something'; (4) shows engineering maturity — train/test split, held-out scalability test, honest error-rate reporting, sensitivity heatmap; and (5) is well-structured and readable with a clear flow diagram (Fig 1), a logical production->distribution->growth->benefits/costs narrative, and explicit assumptions. The blend of a learned model (NN) with a classical OR algorithm (Dijkstra) plus a domain-grounded pollution-accounting layer signals breadth. It is not the most rigorous paper, but it is comprehensive, concrete, and clearly communicated — the profile that earns Outstanding."
section_highlights: "Strong structure: Section 1 opens with a single full-page modeling flowchart (Fig 1) mapping the entire pipeline before any math, so a judge immediately sees the architecture. Section 2 builds models in logical dependency order (Production Model -> Policy Intervention as input perturbations -> Equitable Distribution -> Growth Model). The criterion-value tables (Tables 1-2) and pollution-coefficient table (Table 3) give the paper a 'we set concrete parameters' credibility. Section 3 (Model Application) and Section 4 (Benefits and Costs) ground everything in the USA/Nepal case with quoted percentages and a fully worked transport-cost example. Section 5 (Scalability/Adaptability) is a genuine validation chapter with a held-out 20-country error-rate test split by area, and crucially admits the model is worse for large heterogeneous countries — a self-criticism judges reward. Each model is also introduced with its textbook algorithm box (M-P neuron + BP pseudocode; Dijkstra pseudocode), which reads as rigorous even though it is partly boilerplate. The summary page front-loads the headline numbers."
pitfalls_or_limitations: The NN's input features mix incommensurable and weakly-justified variables (categorical climate/topography/land-quality rankings, an arbitrary 1-10 intensity index) with little explanation of how categoricals were encoded — the 'limit values' and 'criterion values' (e.g. intensity 6.0/4.7/5.3, chemicals 251.5/150.9/75.4) appear without derivation

The transcribed figures are internally inconsistent / implausible: USA total food production value shown as 158 (Fig 10) and ~380M USD elsewhere, Nepal 'output value maximum nearly 50 million dollars' — orders of magnitude and units are not reconciled, suggesting the value scaling is shaky

BP neural net for ~782 samples with 8 hidden neurons risks overfitting; the husbandry sub-model is openly unstable ('prediction error is large') yet still used, and the train/test plots (Figs 17-18) show predicted == actual almost exactly, which is suspicious rather than reassuring

Heavy reliance on idealized assumptions the authors themselves flag: donors help 'for nothing' / voluntarily, all hungry people are in least-developed countries, every country shares one tech-progress and population-growth rate — these strip out the real political economy of food aid

Dijkstra is used to pick beneficiaries by raw distance only; it ignores capacity constraints, multiple donors per receiver, spoilage, and actual logistics networks — it is really a nearest-neighbor assignment dressed as shortest-path, and a transport assignment/transportation-LP would be the proper tool

Pollution model coefficients (Table 3 w-values) are asserted with no source or calibration; the Air/Water/Land sums are linear in chemicals only, ignoring overproduction's land/biodiversity damage which is hand-waved as 'long and lasting'
The Food Donations Function is described conceptually (Fig 23) but never given an explicit equation or fitted parameters, so the 120,500-ton donation figure feeling is unverifiable
Weaknesses section is thin (two short bullets) and does not engage the unit/scale inconsistencies or the husbandry instability that are the model's real soft spots
reusable_patterns: Split-the-system pattern: decompose an ambiguous 'global system' into a small number of concretely-modelable sub-systems, model each separately, then aggregate via a common unit (here, dollar value) — a clean template for any sustainability/system problem

Policy-as-input-perturbation pattern: define your model so interventions map to changes in specific input variables, then read benefits/costs straight off the model output — extremely effective for 'evaluate the effect of an intervention' prompts

Criterion/target-value pattern: derive a 'sustainable target' for each lever from a reference group (e.g. average of most-developed countries) and frame optimization as moving each country toward that target — gives a defensible, non-arbitrary objective

Hybrid learned+classical pipeline: a learned model (NN) for the messy estimation half plus a classical OR algorithm (Dijkstra/assignment) for the decision half — pairing these signals breadth and fits production-then-distribution problems generally

Held-out scalability test with honest failure analysis: predict on countries NOT in training, report error rate by a meaningful split (land area), and explain WHY it fails for one group — a cheap, high-credibility validation section
Two-archetype contrast (developed vs developing, big vs small) to make abstract results concrete and to satisfy 'compare across cases' asks with quoted numbers
Proxy-variable substitution when a needed input is unobservable (use planting development to stand in for livestock feed access) — a reusable move for data-poor features
Bridge function to convert between mismatched quantities (value -> volume via a Donations Function) so one model's output can feed another model's input
